Hi Kameron,
Thanks for another fun Saturday. I'm glad to see a bit more bite here than in your last Saturday (in March), but I'm not making any BARBETS on we get a real SCARE from you. HASTA luego.

Hi Kameron,
Thanks for another fun Saturday. I'm glad to see a bit more bite here than in your last Saturday (in March), but I'm not making any BARBETS on we get a real SCARE from you. HASTA luego.
I found that very difficult.

I had a lot of trouble recalling sooner what finally came to me later. And this puzzle had some terrific clues, like "Walk on water" for GANGPLANK.

But this puzzle also felt unbalanced in the distribution of hard and easy clues. I think not just me but many experienced solvers would find the upper left area of this puzzle to be way too easy for a Saturday puzzle. And then I ran into some difficulty grokking some harder clues, and then we also have MARLSTONE and DARTH (as title for fictional lords *plural*??? I had no idea), and clues I found really tough like "Shell, say" and the clue for FONTANE, of whom I don't recall having heard.
Lovely puzzle! Just delightful! I didn’t know the Swedish model, but I’ll try to remember
Excellent Saturday. Gave me a workout. Although it's infuriating to read that Kameron designed this puzzle in just over twice the time it took me to solve it!
Marlstone crossed with author of "Gashlycrumb Tinies"?  Seriously???

What some ________ get, briefly = GEDS

Is there any category of person that can’t go in that blank?

I had fun though, good challenge

This was harder than any of the APTESTS I took in high school, though I did all mine as Sophomore and Junior, none as a Senior.

I had a Natick with GOREY/MARLSTONE, which was exacerbated by my conflicting belief that 37D was DARTH and 37A was bIRThNAME. I tried everything to resolve it until I realized that the bird was called MUDLARK and not MaDLARK because NEaROsurgery isn't a thing.

One of the AP classes I took was AP Chem. One of my favorite concepts was that of a limiting REACTANT. (If you are making water from 3 moles of hydrogen and 2 moles of oxygen, the hydrogen is the limiting reactiant). But I use that phrase a lot in non chemistry usage. 

For example, when I travel, I usually maintain a very tight schedule. Let's say I have a 7 PM dinner reservation and I'm going to a National Historic Site that closes at 5 PM. If it's a 3 hour drive from the NHS to the restaurant, the dinner reservation is the limiting reactant. I need to plan my day around arriving at the restaurant by 7 PM. But if it's a 1 hour drive, the NHS is the limiting reactant. I need to plan my day around arriving at the NHS before 5 PM
I, for one, would prefer that the New Yorker constructors stay where they are more easily ignored. This was more trivia quiz than crossword.

Great puzzle! One of those ones where I thought I was done for on the first pass but it slowly came together. The clue for DOGBREATH was my favorite.

Crossing MARLSTONE with GOREY was pretty mean though, had to run the alphabet on that one.

So it never ceases to amaze me how a grid that seems insurmountable at first suddenly falls into place. It’s why I keep telling new solvers to be patient.  I had nothing, absolutely nothing in the top half, except maybe a bastardized version of RIGVEDA that I couldn’t spell if my life depended on it. Then I had (incorrectly) MATEINTWO and TSARS which at least got me MARINA and eventually MIMOSA and the rest of that corner, Then it was Ghostbusters to the rescue with ECTOPLASM which opened up that corner despite having NONE before BOTH. In the NW for “settled above stools” I had lots of versions of toushie, etc., but BAD… (BADGUYS? BADDIES?) along with the R of RIG led me to BAR something and eventually BARBETS. Eventually the middle came which got me an in to the NE despite having OBGYN before NEURO as my first guess.
So folks, just be fearless, be open, wait for that bit of inspiration and those few letters that make sense and suddenly even the hardest puzzles can be revealed to you. And this was no walk in the park.
Thanks Cameron for a great workout. Loved DOGBREATH and BARBETS (with BEERMATS in the other corner) and GANGPLANK and LATTEART. And ENVIES over GREED. Speaking of which, it’s a sin that you can put this together in an hour, there ought to be a special circle in Dante’s hell for you where you have to push an OED to the top of a mountain only to have it fall again, or maybe have to write clue after clue for OREO each one original or you will fall in a vat of acid.

Ooh tough! 
Finally got there but with a few lucky and (un)educated guesses…never heard of GEDS, GOREY or HOTLANTA. 
As for models’ names…do we have to? (Unless I know the answer).

Excellent!! That's the KAC I know and fear 😀 This made me fight tooth-and-nail for just about every single clue, except maybe for 31D, RAILCAR ... which turned out to be MARINAS so nvm.

The rest of this weekend I will spend casually trying to inject the NYTimes crossword into casual conversation so i can boast about my ti... nope, can't do that either.😉

No one was more surprised than I by the happy music, without even any flyspeckiing.

I was scrambling the whole time, especially in the center.  SOAPSTARS, HEADPHONE, STRONGSTOMACH...these were brilliant, in my opinion.

Nice Saturday.  Easy enough for someone like me to get it, hard enough to please almost everyone here.  

Doesn't get much better than that.
I've added this to my POY nomination list. Our third KAC treat this year so far, which almost makes up for that hiatus. But not quite. More, please!

MARLSTONE and GOREY intersection had me for 10+ minutes. I couldn’t find any other square that had a chance to be wrong. I didn’t go through typing in every letter of the alphabet. I went through it in my head and no letter made sense a couple times through. Then, I put our in there in my head and it was like “wait… it works for both directions.” so I put the R in. Lo and behold: that sweet music.

Thank you for almost 37 minutes of sweet torture. Again, I got what I wish for with a Saturday puzzle. There was no way I was going to finish it. Not only that, while I had almost all of the middle and the SE, the other three quadrants were mostly blank.

This is why I love doing crossword puzzles. “Maybe, this is that! Oooo! Now, maybe this is that!” I really loved this puzzle. Thank you.

Ye gods, I did it! I was able to complete the puzzle on my own, with just two post-hoc lookups to confirm my educated guesses of PAULINA and FONTANE.

The last square I filled in, with much confusion, was the crossing of MA_LSTONE and GO_EY. I've never heard of either of the entries. Having run the alphabet I arrived at an R there. I checked what MARL STONE is called in Polish: it's "margiel", and I'm unfamiliar with that, too, even though geography (which includes geology) was one of my fave subjects at school 🤷🏽.

The accumulation of proper names and trivia in the middle of the grid was almost too much for me to handle, so I'm really proud of not giving in to the temptation to Google my way out of there.

The NW and SE corners I found tough, too, and I filled them in in pencil. The only area I thought was relatively easy was the NE.

I enjoyed the solve. It felt like a proper Saturday to me - very hard, almost impossibly so, but ultimately doable with an open mind. The clueing was appropriately misleading without being obnoxiously opaque. Nice.

I nearly gave up in total frustration after 55 minutes with only two-thirds of the puzzle done. Stuck with it, got some late help from a family member and heard the happy music after 1:28.  Ultimately had fun.

Being from MA and the right age to follow The Cars, I was looking for Czech, not Swedish.

But definitely not a complaint since that dispute meant a trip to Wikipedia, where I learned about yet another chapter in Ms Porizkova’s rather remarkable life, this one involving Olof Palme.
That was brutal. Marlstone is new for me.

This was my favorite type of puzzle: the kind I nearly despaired of completing… but soldiered on to hear the music. So satisfying! 

We all have our idiosyncratic knowledge bases— I was unfamiliar with CLEM and FONTANE, but was somewhat surprised when others were stumped by GOREY or the stunningly beautiful PAULINA Porizkova. Not to diss those who may have struggled with those answers, or to pat myself on the back for knowing them, but I find it fascinating how much clever folks’ “gimmes” can differ! What I do find disheartening is those who seemingly resent the inclusion of anything/anyone they have not personally encountered. All of us have areas of ignorance, and (hopefully!) areas of expertise. And we all should be willing to own our ignorance, and be grateful for what we can learn from these puzzles. 

Thanks, Kameron, for some fun worthy of a Saturday!

commentBody:
Are there so few Cars fans here?  Impossible for me not to think of Ric Ocasek without his partner Paulina Porizkova in the 80s and 90s.  They were everywhere, weren't they?

Lovely Saturday.  First-pass scratched surface led to fear of last Saturday and having my streak broken at six.  But then, inch by inch, slowly I turned, etc.

Upon solving I had a second of satori.

What makes a clue/fill set acceptably obscure?  If a certain subset of humans in the world might well be expected to find it unobscure based on education, livelihood, culture, religion, etc. and not based on niche obsessions. 

Take MARLSTONE.  I might whine it's a TIL but there must be thousands of contractors and engineers and construction workers who would know it immediately.

So, good obscure.

As opposed to last Saturday's Back to the Future II clue whose fill was UCLA.  Niche niche niche.

Not even rabid, generic cinephiles would know this one.

Bad obscure.

I therefore propose that if the fill is knowable only to frothing becostumed fans and first-online (on line) purchasers of tickets to its convention, it is to be regarded as bad obscure.

Yes, yes "rabid" and "nice" are squishily defined here, but the gist should be clear.

Obscure knowledge gleaned from somewhere in mainstream life = good obscure.

Obscure knowledge gleaned from fringe pursuits like Back to the Future board games and dating sites = bad obscure.

This was a real wrestling match for me but I eventually got it without help.  I had badBREATH in for 16A and denIES for 19A for the longest time so my NW corner was a mess.  It’s been a long road to build the lateral thinking skills to be able to solve a good end-of-week puzzle, but it’s very satisfying to succeed!

Finally managed to complete it in over one hour but it was a pretty unenjoyable one hour. 


I usually love a good Saturday challenge but today, a lot of the fill and cluing felt like it was made difficult for difficulty's sake. Anyway, serves me right for constantly complaining about recent weekend puzzles being too easy.

Anyone care to join the RIGVEDA and GOREY Society? We meet monthly to compare and contrast texts from these two classic sources. From a recent meeting:

“Lover of song, may these our songs on every side encompass thee:
Strengthening thee of lengthened life, may they be dear delights to thee.”

“M is for Maud who was swept out to sea,
N is for Neville who died of ennui.”
Yikes. The trivia here was particularly eclectic and esoteric all at once. Tough one.
Gee whiz.

That was a well-done puzzle, for mine. Lots of things I didn’t know I could guess – although I’ve seen others say that on puzzles that frustrated the hell out of me.
 
The stick in my case was 27D, 29D and 31A – which I reckon I would have got without checking had I gone with my gut that 48A was HASTA. (I know no Spanish.) Instead stuck to the conviction that 30D ended with an S, so was never going to see LATTE ART.
 
Otherwise a perfect challenge level, taking between 20 and 30 minutes, which is where I like my Saturdays to be.

Hmmm…. How to put this? 

I had _A_INAS and i misread the clue as “Birth places” instead of “Berth places”. Aha, I thought! The NYT has overcome its prudishness! I smugly added a V_G… Turns out i was not in the right ballpark!
So that's what passing a kidney stone feels like.
Fun accompaniment for today's fun Midi: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=uq-gYOrU8bA

Difficult. 
I'm well-educated and well-read but I didn't know Edward Gorey, so I've learnt that one. As several here have discussed, an individual's knowledge is .... individual. I knew RIG VEDA but didn't know about BAR BETS!
I love the clues for DOG BREATH and AVIARIES.

This is the kind of puzzle that I used to find very intimidating. (And sometimes still do, to be honest.) With such distinct sections, you can’t rely on crosses to get you from one area to another. But I’ve learned to take a deep breath and plunge in, because sometimes just a little knowledge and a few lucky guesses are all you need to get started. For me, these were BAR BETS and DOG BREATH (big leap of faith) in the NE, SOAP STARS , GOREY and PAULINA in the middle, RONAN and TSARS in the SW, and ECTOPLASM and BEER MAT in the SE.  From there, it was a process of intuition highlighted by amusing finds. (HEADPHONE! GANGPLANK! And what’s cuter than a PANDA CAR?)

This is why I come here.

Would there have been complaints if 13 A was clued as [Places to put barbets] and 16 A was clued as [Barbets secret weapon]?  I would have liked that.  My thanks to Caitlin for pointing out that connection.

This is my favorite type of puzzle.  It had many things I know nothing about but the answers could be reasoned out.  It wasn't as tough as some of the older KAC offerings, but still put up a good fight.  I'm a happy solver today.

Amazing grid. Great stacked middle section. As Debbie used to write: do what you need to do to enjoy completing the puzzle. I googled for rig Veda, Paulina and Fontane to give me footholds in 3 sections . Favorites were dog breath, loud shirts and soap stars (recalling Susan Lucci /Erica for all us old All My Children fans)!
A SAND SNAKE, a MUD LARK and a PANDA CAR walk into a bar…
Edward Gorey and Paulina Porizkova were gimmes, for me. Payback for all the Star Wars/Trek, sports, rap and chess answers that weren’t.
I thought "Tac preceder" must be TIC, but at the same time, it seemed too obvious… eventually I realized my intuition was right and it didn't work.
I haven't even looked at the puzzle yet. I just have to post my excitement for a NYT KAC Saturday. 🥳

I am not a fast solver and if I posted my actual times you would see that, but I do take satisfaction in completing today's puzzle in 53% less than average time. When I first saw the grid design, I would not have predicted that.  Some fresh fill.

Ack, it's KAC! Welcome back, Kameron. The only two things I was 100 percent positive about on my first pass through were RONAN and PAULINA (was not thrown by Swedish). Gradually everything else revealed itself, but twas a battle.
Perhaps my favorite leap was DOGBREATH, which reminded me of Belker from Hill Street Blues*.

*couldn't find a clip of him saying it. :(
Let's be careful out there.

Settling down with my cuppa,
rubbing the sleep from my fuzzy eyes
and opening up a sea of white
and it’s a KAC.

Sweet start, Sir Saturday.

The solve was an odd one for me.

NW held up by BARtabS and treats 
(so was waiting for sloth or wrath)…

I *never* like to leave the NW unfinished,
but I know when I gotta move on.

SW opened up nicely,
then the other corners until
middle just blew wide open.

Loved it.
Tough to break in but went reasonable quickly once I did. Only one look up: RIG VEDA as that was totally new to me. Fun solve!

Well, hello KAC.

Hello to no-knows, misdirects, wit, humor, a slew of answers never seen in the box before, and a junk-free grid despite seas of white.

To be more specific:
•	Delightful and interesting answers – DOG BREATH, PANDA CARS, GANG PLANK, LOUD SHIRT, DIRTY NAME, HOTLANTA, STRONG STOMACH.  
•	That astounding stagger-stack of seven answers that not only includes five NYT answer debuts, but two vertical answers that cross the entire stack – that are debuts as well!  What?  Huh?

And the thing you can’t quantify – that unmistakable KAC personality that inhabits the air around you as you uncover squares.

My brain got to “Whee!” in one splat-fill area, and, in another, got to gloriously grind at an impervious block of diamond until (cue angelic chorus) the tiniest sliver of light emerged.  

Cameron, you are an original with remarkable talent.  Your puzzles astound, entertain, and wow me every time.  Thank you so much for this one!

This one was quite a workout, especially after KAC's last puzzle was uncharacteristically easy. If I had the luxury of leaving a puzzle undone and coming back to it later, I may have been able to complete it. But in my set ways, the puzzle is the next-to-last thing I do before calling it a night (er, early morning), in the kitchen, pen and paper, hovering over my warm milk and a couple of meds. 

But I'm a creature of habit. I did start it a bit earlier, knowing that historically, a Friday would take me longer, and in that first run I got very little. I knew Gorey, because when I lived in NY we were fortunate to have joined a club that gave us free tickets to B'way and off-B'way shows, and we were there for the Opening - and Closing night of Gorey Stories, a storied Broadway flop. I think we left during intermission, it was that bad.

I was so pleased that my diligence got three of the quadrants and the center filled in, albeit in a lengthy process. BARBETS was a gimme, until it wasn't, and it was that NW that finally did me in. I had no knowledge of RIGVEDA, and AVIARIES and GEDS just wouldn't come to me. But my last act before closing down my computer session is to come here and read the column and comments, so I don't have the good fortune of going back to give the puzzle one more try, since I've seen all the answers. 

As dejected as I am for the rare occasion of not have completed a puzzle, I'm thrilled that for once this was a worthy challenge. Hoping for more of them.
Needed way too much help on this one but that has to do with being distracted with work stress.

Good puzzle for a Saturday. I was stuck in the southeast corner area for a while, thinking BOTH was NONE (for the refusal to choose), and that the ghostly substance must start with ECHO (it was ECTOPLASM).

Very fine Saturday puzzle. Solved without assistance, but took 34 minutes. Didn't know GOREY, but MARLSTONE seemed clear from the clue. Never heard of a MUDLARK and didn't know CLEM, but the L seemed the only choice for that crossing. Didn't know FONTANE, but the crosses all came easily. Similarly for RONAN.

Getting DOGBREATH/RIGVEDA instantly gave me confidence that this one wouldn't be too bad. DIRTYWORD rather than DIRTYNAME slowed me down a bit.

"Walk on water?" for GANGPLANK was cute. Thanks for a nice Saturday puzzle.
Very difficult, but generally fair. DOGBREATH was terrific, and for that I'll overlook AVONREP. Maybe people use that term today, but for me it's always been Avon Lady.
This was insanely hard. No idea how I finished this without any lookups. The references and cluing were sheer brutality. I’m going back to bed.
29D completely ruined the entire grid. Totally unsolvable without looking this name up due to the crosses. This would have been a good challenge otherwise.

I hope that folks unfamiliar with the work of Edward Gorey consider checking it out. They could start with The Doubtful Guest or The Ghastlycrumb Tinies. Gorey was unique. 

I enjoyed the puzzle today as much as everyone else. It took me every minute of an hour but in the end even the NW (SeaTac country) yielded to persistence.
The title sequence to the Mystery series on PBS featured a delightful animation of Edward Gorey’s art. https://youtu.be/CwrIZds3nVM?si=pK7XxLAvqY1tY_kG
I took one look at this grid pattern and almost gave up on the spot.

I found the puzzle to be challenging and fun, and I was thrilled to finally get through after struggling for a long time on the central region. 

Question, though: I remember hearing (probably here) that a long-standing NYT crossword rule is no repetition, neither in the questions or the answers, so why did two clues include  "epithet"?
NW corner took me forever as I was convinced that "the original side hustle" was MARYKAY, which is close, but very wrong.
No one more surprised than me when I finally solved this one.  Great job!

What a fun challenge. With all that open space I was surprised to come in five minutes below my average. Especially after confusing myself with quicklime and crocodile in that vast middle section. The crossword is often an illustration of the sink cost fallacy: when you're stuck jettison what doesn't work.

All I know about GOREY is those weird illustrations and titles, so it took me a while even though I knew it wasn't Edward AbbEY.

The satisfaction of completing a hard one is always a good start to the weekend.

Really tough one for me. Twelve debut answers, and more than a couple of those that were not terribly familiar. Had to look up more than a few things - probably shouldn't count it. Oh well. A couple of quite interesting puzzle finds today. Here's one: A Sunday from May 12, 2013 by David J. Kahn with the title: "Simply Put." First theme clue and answer: "With 44-, 63-, 77- and 93-across, a long-winded piece of advice." POTENTIALCONSEQUENCES And the rest of those referenced answers, in order: OFAPLANORDECISIONONE CANNOTREVERSESHOULDBE HEEDEDPRIORTOTHETIME ANACTIONISEFFECTUATED And then... 24-down: "This puzzle's long-winded advice, simply put." LOOKBEFOREYOULEAP Here's that link: https://www.xwordinfo.com/Crossword?date=5/12/2013&g=24&d=D I'll put another puzzle in a reply. ....
Trivia central today.

Great Saturday!

I was mostly breezing through this one, apart from a blank square here and there (such as in GOREY, CLEM, and MUDLARK)…until the time came when I could no longer ignore the upper-right corner, which turned out to be a “proper Saturday” mini-puzzle within the puzzle for me.

And that upper-right corner is golden! I had absolutely no clue on RIGVEDA, and the only entry in the entire section that was a gimme for me was ERRED. I can’t believe how long it took me to pick up on DOGBREATH, but I had to smile at that one, and everything else fell pretty quickly after that.

Aside from RIGVEDA, everything in that corner looks so obvious in retrospect — there’s really nothing even remotely stretchy!

So the other day, some of us were talking about synchronicity. 

Today, I’ve experienced it in reverse. I’m staying at a B & B this weekend, and did the puzzle last night, straining to dislodge the name of Edward GOREY from the deepest recesses of my brain. 

Today I discover that this place has a jigsaw puzzle of one of GOREY’s art works available for guests to do.
Lots of fun, although initially I didn’t hold out much hope of solving it. It did all eventually fall into place though to my surprise!

Man, I went down the 3-card Monte rabbit hole this morning on YouTube and Google. So fascinating to watch. Wikipedia has a really well written post on this with pictures and examples. Didn’t know this had been researched so well.

I fell for all the tricks!  
42A:  didn't know the guy, but -ONAN suggested CONAN as the name, making the fictional lords PATCH CATCH WATCH MATCH?
And of course LOVE SHIRT was easy!
ORTHO--a natural!  All old people think of ORTHOedists all the time, as we stumble and bumble.
Oh, goodie!  German author! Ancient Hindu hymns!  Easy Peasy!
MARLvelous clues and entries!
I thought of many a DIRTY WORD, Kam, with you in mind.
Bless your heart.

Having just been in SEA-Tac (twice--still recovering)  I can attest that there are cities smaller and less busy than that huge, bustling airport.
Very difficult. Had to do a lot of guessing but somehow solved it with no lookups.
This was quite a bad one tbh, especially the clue for 'precedes Tac'' was a bit ridiculous. Very hard, and not in a good way. Didn't feel rewarding figuring out the clues.

I’m sorry but barbets over Bartabs is so dumb, talk about outdated and lame. Even as a Saturday’s, too many obscure and niche terms that are reaches. Hard only due to obscurity is boring and not satisfying
Beautiful grid and a satisfyingly challenging puzzle. It was great perusing the comments and finding no complaints about its being too easy.

Admittedly, English native speaker but francophone here, and pretty sure the feminine petite elides the final E when followed by a vowel, regardless of gender, to petit amie. Similar to bel amie. One of those things you learn early on that makes you wonder how you're able to recognize any gender or plural in French when speaking haha. I think this cluing is wrong here and misleading. Any native francophones here to provide insight?
This felt like an extended inside joke that I was not in on. Not my jam today.

I almost never do this, but instead of looking for a section I could solve, today I went clue by clue looking for ANY WORD that could get me into the puzzle.  I got in at MIMOSA -- all the way towards the bottom.

I'm too tired now to list all the things I didn't know.  I'm sure they're mostly the same things you didn't know.  Writeovers:  I had some sort of --T heART for the foam designs and PRAIRIE before THE WEST for where the wagons went.  I misread BERTH places for BIRTH places and therefore had VAGINAS where MARINAS should have gone.  Don't ask.

I thought the channel blocker would be something like a gallSTONE, when it was HEADPHONE.  At one point I almost put in HEADSTONE when HEAD was coming in, but it didn't fit the clue.  Don't ask.

I ended up not being able to finish the NW.  Please don't ask me about 2D (what a DOOK of an answer!) and 3D.  I'm in no mood to discuss that fiendish section of the puzzle.  If you bring it up, I'll shoot my ECTOPLASM at you and call you a DIRTY NAME.

I needed to consult Google more than once to solve this one. I certainly can't complain about it being too easy. I appreciated the Saturday challenge but some of the clues felt like forced associations rather than pleasingly obtuse. I don't know how to describe it any better than that. For example, 26A (what some prisoners earn, in brief). 37A is another (many an epithet). 

That being said, I do applaud the effort and overall enjoyed the solve.

The average of the commentary seems about right; puzzle was trivia-heavy but at least somewhat clever; likely to be frustrating for some people (a little to me, too, though I couldn't go so far as to say it was Natick-y). Maybe slightly light on revelations. It was OK?

Great Saturday puzzle! Difficult and fun. So little to get on the first pass. Ah, the Southeast gave me a couple of entries. AMIE and RONAN, and then DARTH and TSARS. OK, I'm on my way. But all these long answers, and I haven't a "clue". The clues are too tricky! But you can get part of them! Something SNAKE and something STONE and something STOMACH (Why isn't it "cast iron" STOMACH? Keep working on it! "Nevertheless, she persisted." The beautifully clued pair of things that get "settled atop stools" and things that can "knock you out". Mwah! Chef's kiss perfection. And, after hard work, we are rewarded with the music. A big thank you to Edward GOREY, who provided me with a major entry into the HEART of the puzzle. His "Gashlycrumb Tinies" was a poster on my dorm wall in day's long past. https://onehundredpages.wordpress.com/2015/03/17/the-gashlycrumb-tinies-a-very-gorey-alphabet-book/ Great puzzle. Thanks, Kameron Austin Collins! Give us more like this!
I thought the puzzle was challenging, clever, and fair. A perfect Saturday.

"Tourist nickname for a Southern mecca..."
Redneck Riviera did not fit.

This puzzle was quite a challenge; it seems there was at least one complete unknown for every solver. Mine was the 19th Century German author. The only one I know is Goethe.
This felt insanely tricky to crack into, but I solved bang-on my average time - so it can’t have been too bad! Greatly enjoyed LATTEART once it clicked but I did have mATThEART for far too long…
Tough one! Had to take the doggo for a walk and come back to it (and a second cup of coffee). Doggo approved!
This one actually really really ticked me off. Unnecessarily hard. You can do better.
LOgoSHIRT slowed me down.

Genuine question: At what point do you admit that you're just not intelligent enough for grown-up crosswords? I've been doing them regularly/seriously for about a year and doing the occasional puzzle for most of my 40+ years of living. After putting in 40 minutes of genuine effort on this one, I had less than half filled in. I broke down and used Check Puzzle and saw that only about half of that was actually correct. And I had no idea where to go next. At that point I gave up.
I gather that this isn't normal. I regularly see posts from people who aren't even American (NOT that American = smart, I only say that because there are many American references in the puzzles) or native English speakers who by 2-3 months after doing their first ever crossword are regularly solving the hard ones in 20-30 minutes. Something seems wrong with me. At what point do you admit that improvement isn't coming?

I’m of two minds on this one.

While I loved some of the longer answers and their clues (DOG BREATH, HOTLANTA, AVIARIES) I was also mildly displeased with some of the crossings and proper noun/trivia fill (GOREY/MARLSTONE.)

I think that in the end, the proper nouns turned this into a “no thank you” for me, in spite of some otherwise excellent and fun misdirects. 1A started off strong (even if I had “tabs” instead of BETS) but the rest of the puzzle felt like it had too many shoehorned answers to flow nicely.

Great, chewy puzzle, thanks, KAC. I'm really surprised at how many people stumbled at MARLSTONE and GOREY. Edawrd Gorey is very well known, for his illustrations for Mystery! https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=CwrIZds3nVM and his macabre satiric alphabet of child deaths, the Tinies https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Gashlycrumb_Tinies Dark humour with whimsical illustration, what's not to like? MARLSTONE? There are lots of towns and streets (and cigarettes) named after marl. Many decades ago, I was working for an environmental agency and sometimes had to take phone calls from complaining citizens. One lady called in who was absolutely incensed that there were open marl pits at the edge of town, they were so dangerous and kids could drown, and . . . It is amazing how, after more than a decade of daily solving, I still keep having new experiences with these puzzles. Today was the first time I'd ever gone well over halfway down a grid without so much as a guess. And my first confident answer was even farther down, almost at the bottom.

Expecting the downs to be easier, I was surprised to have almost the same experience, getting down to "The Gashlycrumb Tinies" before having a confident answer. Slowly, slowly worked my way back up the grid, only to realize I still had a completely empty top left quadrant with only 6D and 7D filled in. And those didn't make sense. (I had "tic" for 7D.) So how am I now done in my usual time, no research?  

I can only say that the confidence one builds over years of solves must help. Rather than despair with that empty corner and no new access points, experience whispers that if I keep scanning over the grid, eventually something new will occur. (Such as SEA TAC.)

And that "eventually" comes with a time warp. I only know how long it took when I come up for air and scroll up to see my time. Could have been an hour, could have seemed to be negative minutes.

It's interesting to see here in the Comments those for whom Edward GOREY was a gimme and those for whom he was a complete unknown. I have long loved his "Gashlycrumb Tinies", a tremendously eccentric abecedarium, and there was a time when I knew them all by heart. For those who don't know it, I encourage you to take a look. Its humor and aesthetic are idiosyncratic, but there are those of us who love it: https://onehundredpages.wordpress.com/2015/03/17/the-gashlycrumb-tinies-a-very-gorey-alphabet-book/ For those who love it, I'll ask you to reply with your favorite letter of GOREY's alphabet. Mine? "N is for Neville who died of ennui." Followed closely by, "B is for Basil assaulted by bears."

FWIW, for new solvers (or those complaining about the run of challenging Saturdays), here’s how I tackled the NW, for me the hardest section of the puzzle. Maybe this can help those solvers understand how some of us use the crosses to solve even when we don’t know the answers right off the bat.

I had filled in SEA for 7D on my first pass: I ruled out the obvious “tac” figuring that 1A was a plural ending in S.

But other than that and “S” and “ATH” at the ends of 13A and 16A (from 14D and 17D), I was staring at a sea of white space. 

I tried deNIES for 19A and BABIES for 4D. I wasn’t certain about either, and of course I ended up being wrong about 19A — but that sort of let’s-take-a-stab-and-see-what-happens is often the way I get a toehold in a tricky section. 

I had already thought 5D might be ERRED, and since I didn’t have anything else I figured what the hell and put it in.

Now things started to fall into place. I had ___BR_ATH for 16A, which made it easy to see BREATH. Aha — we’re talking dogs not pugilists: DOGBREATH (chuckle).

The E made 6D down obvious as TIES.  

Now I had ___ARIES for 13D, which led to AVIARIES. 

That gave me _ADd__S for 1D. I put in BADdieS without conviction. 

Still the B led to BARBETS for 1A. AVOe___ at 2D had to be AVONREP. So 19A was not denIES but ENVIES (facepalm). Aha: BADEGGS not BADdies for 1D. 

I had already had SPA in mind as likely, and GR_ED was obviously GREED (another facepalm).

The D in GEDS/RIGVEDA was the last to fall.

A good tough Saturday grid.

I have never seen a chess puzzle which challenged one to find the " mate in one".

That's just a move that checkmates.  

Much more interesting are puzzles with mate in two, mate in three, etc, because the losing player must have no other options than to move in a way that progresses the mating combination.  Every move has to be the only option left for the losing player.

I felt weird about the clue "Begrudges" for ENVIES. I do not think the word "envy" means "begrudge", not even slightly.

To envy a person is to wish one's own situation were like that person's in some respect. The word "envy" does not carry any sense of resentment (per se!).

This is a frequent problem with inaccurate clueing: Merely because envy can sometimes be *accompanied by* resentment of the other person, the clue mistakenly assumes this to be part of a definition of "envy".

While you're here, I'll ask something I've been pondering since watching the PBS Nova hour on Quantum topics.

Do classical chemistry and physics explain how combustion produces light?  The alchemists knew that different materials produced different colored flames but did an explanation of the flame itself require the idea of photons? 

Not asking for a friend.  I've been wondering about this for a few days without motivation to do any research.  Besides, it's 3am and I just finished the puzzle via the step away method.

@Al  in Pittsburgh

That is a *really* interesting question, the more so as it is kind of an obvious one.

I don't recall ever hearing a pre-quantum* explanation of the interaction of light and matter and chemical reactions like combustion.  Even though I'm not really as knowledgable about the history of physics or chemistry as a lot of my colleagues, I would think I would have heard something.

The only time I can recall even brushing against the idea is for the photoelectric effect.  When a beam of light falls on the surface of a metal, electrons can be made to fly off the surface. 

Classical physics predicts that when light falls onto a metal, the energy becomes part of the energy of the "free" electrons flowing around in the metal.  When enough light energy is pumped into that sea of electrons, some get so energetic as to fly away from the metal.  Classical theory predicted, incorrectly, that the photoelectric effect did not depend on the frequency of light, but only the total energy.

Not really sure if I answered the question, but it did cause me to think quite a bit, which was pleasant for a change.

*I assume by classical you mean prior to quantum mechanics.

@Al  in Pittsburgh

On a similar note, whenever I'm sitting around a fire (a camp fire or fire pit) with a chemist or physicist, I like to ask them to explain, "What is fire?"
They usually start with something about rapid oxidation emitting energy, and I have to ask again, "No, I mean the 'stuff' of the flame.  What is it?  And why does it have the color and shape that it has?"
I then get something about "plasma" that I don't fully understand, and respond, "So, it's a different state of matter, just like the Ancient Greeks thought?"

@Francis @The X-Phile Thanks for the thoughtful responses. Yes, I used "classical" to mean "pre-quantum. I had been leaning toward the opinion that explaining light from combustion required the newer model. Even Maxwell's equations which deal only with electromagnetic energy and waves, don't explicitly address the creation of light despite the clever T-shirts. https://tinyurl.com/yf2xmkkf It is also interesting to note, as X-Phile does, that the ancients in both Europe and Asia independently considered Fire to be one of the foundational forms of earthly matter. These became integral components of classification of human personalities and systems of Astrology. Earth, Air, Fire, and Water in the West. Earth, Fire, Wood, Metal, and Water in the East. One can get all metaphysical about whether that difference shows one group more abstract and the other more practical, but that is the subject for another time and place.

@The X-Phile

That is a hard question to answer precisely.  

I would say that fire are like clouds.  The flames are places where the atoms and molecules are boosted into higher energy states.  When they jump back to the ground state, they release photons in the visual range.  Where the flame isn't it's not quite hot enough, it doesn't.  That gives the shape, which changes wildly as wind currents mix things up.

Likewise, clouds are really where atmospheric conditions are just right for water to condense.  There are water vapor molecules where the cloud isn't seen, but conditions are just not right for forming water droplets, which scatter light, which we call clouds.

So, fire is a process in which there is so much energy flowing around that electrons jump into less stable orbits, and emit light when they fall back down.

Much of the yellow light we see is because of sodium.  Sodium atoms have a transition from a p to and s state, which produces light in the yellow region, the famous sodium doublet.

You make a great mistake on getting me onto these topics.  I love writing about science and math.

It's interesting to think about the issue of "good vs. bad obscurities", but, unsurprisingly, I don't agree with your analysis. 
Obviously, each solver has a unique history, which leads to different stuff that is easy and "trivial".  If something is unknown to me, but well-known to some subset of solvers, does that make it good/acceptable?
Just about every good, hard puzzle has something that I don't have a clue about:  a person, an archaic fact, a song that I've never heard of.  Some of these are fun to learn, some I forget as soon as the puzzle is done.  Thankfully, I can almost always infer the answer from the crosses.

@EagleAngle Here's a counterpoint which may cheer you up. 

First off, please don't pay much attention to anyone's self-reported completion time. There's no way to verify it. Or if they looked anything up. Etc.

Except for tournaments, it's not a race. Some may enjoy speed runs, while others enjoy smelling the proverbial roses. Some enjoy stopping to admire certain clues, or think about something just learned. 

I haven't been able to find a solid estimate of total number of NYT puzzle solvers, but one estimate that made some sense is 2 million people. That's a large number, but is a fraction of potential players. So you're already in a limited group who choose the harder NYT puzzles vs the many other options. Then consider that on any given day there are only a few hundred who comment here. So there's really no need to feel discouraged that your time might be less than the time of someone who's in a demographic representing a fraction of a percent of total potential solvers.

As a last note, there are sites which exist solely to help solve difficult clues so people can finish the puzzle. If your difficulty in solving certain puzzles was an outlier, these sites wouldn't exist.

So worry not, friend. Just being here is a good thing. Take all the time you need and don't let difficult grids get you down. Wishing you a great week of puzzle-solvkng ahead.
